_meta.note: Built to the Armagh-locked standard. Cork is the heaviest Munster file: Munster finalists (lost to Kerry) who then went on an unbeaten All-Ireland series run, beating Meath and shocking Donegal away to reach the All-Ireland quarter-final, where Mayo ended them. Six championship games, full scorer detail on the games where it was published.
_meta.verification: All scorelines, venues and referees verified against primary match reports and official tables (gaa.ie, Munster GAA, RTE, Irish Examiner, The42, Cork GAA, The Journal). This file CORRECTS the stale per_county_canonical source, which had Cork's All-Ireland run wrong (it predated these games).

manager.name: John Cleary
manager.note: Oversaw a season that brought Cork back to an All-Ireland quarter-final for the first time since 2023, built on a shrewd tactical plan (with backroom including Kevin Walsh and Kevin Murray) that delivered the shock away win over Donegal. Still could not end Cork's long wait for a Munster title or an All-Ireland semi-final.

league.note: Came through a successful Division 2 league campaign and reached the Division 2 final, losing to Meath who hauled Cork down in the final minute to kill the clock (an incident that prompted a refereeing clarification on Rule 5.42). Promoted to Division 1.

source.snippet: Cast your mind back to the league final and when Meath hauled Cork down in the final minute to kill the clock and deny them a chance to work an equaliser.
All-Ireland Senior Football Championship Quarter-Final Mayo 0-23 Cork 0-18. Cork's 2026 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship campaign came to a disappointing conclusion at Croke Park.
Cork defeated Limerick by 4-16 to 1-16 in the Munster Senior Football Championship Quarter-Final... Scorers for Cork: S Sherlock (0-4); T Walsh, I Maguire, S McDonnell, D Sheedy (1-0 each).

results.note: Four first-half goals blew Limerick away before a strangely mute second half. Cork goalkeeper Micheál Aodh Martín was controversially NOT sin-binned for a foul that conceded a Limerick penalty (referee Seamus Mulhane black-carded Brian O'Driscoll by mistaken identity instead; O'Driscoll was later dismissed on a second yellow). Full scorer list. Limerick's two-pointers (2: Naughton, Coleman) read from the same report.

source.snippet: Semi-Final 25 Apr Tipperary 1-12 4-18 Cork FBD Semple Stadium

results.note: A 15-point semi-final win (the joint-widest margin of the 2026 Munster series, level with the Limerick QF), four goals again, to reach Cork's first Munster final since 2021. Full scorer list not yet captured from a primary report; to be filled on a verification pass.

scorers.note: 1 two-pointer; goal on the hour mark

source.snippet: Cork: M Cronin 0-5 (tp, f), B O'Driscoll 1-2 (tp), S McDonnell 0-2 (tp), C Og Jones 0-2, S Sherlock 0-2 (sl, '45'), C O'Callaghan 0-1, D Buckley 0-1.

results.note: Cork's best chance of a first Munster title since 2012. They led 0-6 to 0-1 early and 0-13 to 0-10 at the break playing with the wind, but David Clifford's 39th-minute goal turned it Kerry's way; O'Driscoll's goal on the hour brought Cork back to within three before Kerry pulled clear. Kerry's sixth Munster title in a row. Full scorer list. Kerry's two-pointers (2: D Clifford, T Brosnan) read from the same report.

scorers.note: sent off on a straight red on 51 minutes

source.snippet: Following defeat to Kerry in the Munster Final, Cork responded with an impressive 0-30 to 1-24 victory over Meath in Páirc Uí Chaoimh.

results.note: Cork's All-Ireland opener as a seeded Munster finalist, a three-point home win over Meath (a rematch of the bad-tempered league final). Colm O'Callaghan was sent off in this game, which ruled him out of the following Donegal tie through suspension. Full scorer list not yet captured from a primary report; to be filled on a verification pass. Date approximate (first weekend of the All-Ireland series, Munster/Connacht finalists).

source.snippet: Scorers for Cork: Steven Sherlock (3 tpf, 1 2pt, 1 '45), Tommy Walsh (1tp), Luke Fahy (1tp) 0-2, Dara Sheedy, Ian Maguire, Sean McDonnell, Conor Corbett 0-1 each.

results.note: One of the great recent Cork results and the signature win of their season. Rank underdogs against the All-Ireland favourites, they came from seven points down (1-10 to 0-6 early in the second half) on a disciplined possession plan, with two-pointers the decisive weapon and Steven Sherlock immense with 0-9. Cork led for the first time only with eight minutes left (Tommy Walsh, then Luke Fahy long-range two-pointers); a Sherlock two-point free in the 65th minute proved the winner. First Cork win in Donegal since 1998; direct passage to the quarter-finals. Crowd 16,600 to 17,223 (reports vary). Five two-pointers for Cork, none for Donegal. Referee: David Gough.

source.snippet: Scorers for Cork: Steven Sherlock 0-7 (1 2pt free, 0-1 '45, 0-1 free), Paul Walsh 0-3, Mark Cronin 0-2 (1 2pt free), Chris Óg Jones 0-2, Ian Maguire 0-1, Colm O'Callaghan 0-1, Rory Maguire 0-1, Brian Hurley 0-1.

results.note: Elimination game. Level at 0-9 each at half-time, but Mayo's second-half scoring power pulled them clear. Cork were left to rue missed chances (post-match analysis highlighted a strong wides tally and possession dominance that went unrewarded). The defeat meant Cork failed to reach the All-Ireland semi-finals for the 14th year running, equalling the worst streak in their history. Full scorer list. Mayo's perspective is held in Mayo's file (Mayo is still alive, semi-finalist). Referee: Martin McNally.

collection_meta.collected_by: Claude (research session, not yet hand-verified by Rory)
fields_not_found: Full scorer lists for the Tipperary Munster semi-final and the Meath All-Ireland Round 1 win (scorelines and key facts verified; full per-player breakdowns to be filled on a verification pass)
Exact date of the Meath Round 1 game (placed on the first All-Ireland weekend, late May 2026)
collection_meta.notes: Cork is the Munster heavyweight and a strong Tier 1 narrative county. The arc is clean and bookend-able: blew teams away in Munster (eight goals across two games), came up short against Kerry again (14 years and counting without a Munster title), then produced the season's signature underdog performance to shock Donegal away before Mayo ended them one round short of a semi-final (14th year running, equalling their worst streak). Steven Sherlock is the standout individual: scorer-in-chief across the whole campaign, 0-9 the difference in Ballybofey. The Donegal away win is a clear signature-panel candidate. This file CORRECTS the stale canonical data, which had Cork beating Donegal 'from 7 down' but mislabelled the round and missed the Meath and Mayo games entirely.
